abstract: Legal protection for investors remains a cornerstone of capital market stability. This study examines the structural shift in oversight within the financial services industry, specifically the transition of supervisory authority from Bapepam-LK to the Financial Services Authority (OJK). Unlike Bapepam-LK, which operated under the Ministry of Finance and reported to the Minister, the OJK functions as an independent body accountable to the House of Representatives and the public. A primary driver behind the OJK’s establishment was the need to address deficiencies in the protection of financial service consumers. Given these regulatory changes, this research analyzes the current framework of legal protection within the capital market and identifies the stakeholders entitled to such protections under Law No. 8 of 1995 on Capital Markets and Law No. 21 of 2011 on the Financial Services Authority.
